title: Can Language Models Compose Skills In-Context?
authors: Not explicitly listed in provided text
research_question: Can language models compose skills in-context, specifically whether they can solve queries for novel composite tasks combining several simple tasks when provided with in-context examples?

methodology.approach: Systematic empirical studies and theoretical analysis
methodology.tasks: Linguistic and logical tasks from XSL designed for probing composition abilities
methodology.key_aspect: Models need to learn novel skills and compositions during inference

key_findings: Simple task examples can hurt performance on composite queries rather than improve it
Models generally do not recognize the composition and do not align simple task examples with corresponding steps
Even Chain-of-Thought (CoT) examples may mismatch skills to wrong steps
Inner attention analysis provides further evidence

theoretical_analysis.finding: Ignorance of compositional structure can harm performance, while aligning examples to appropriate steps can potentially improve it
theoretical_analysis.setting: Stylized setting that captures the essence of in-context composition

proposed_solution.name: Expanded Chain-of-Thought (ExpCoT)
proposed_solution.approach: Views simple task examples as composite task examples with missing steps and expands them into CoT format with missing steps marked by special symbols
proposed_solution.benefit: Explicitly aligns examples with corresponding steps to improve performance

contributions: Systematic experiments investigating in-context composition ability of representative open-source language models
Theoretical analysis explaining empirical observations and revealing that explicitly aligning in-context examples with corresponding steps can help accomplish composite tasks
Proof-of-concept method that significantly improves in-context composition performance
